items.title: The Secretary of Homeland
items.description: T he most important policy decision made by Markwayne Mullin in his short tenure atop the Department of Homeland Security stood for barely a day. ICE officers had just killed two people: a Mexican construction worker with three American sons in Texas, and a Colombian delivery driver whose 3-year-old daughter saw him lying dead in the street in Maine. In each case, the officers had been looking for men other than the ones they killed. DHS officials claimed that the officers had fired defensively during chaotic vehicle stops, but they offered no body-camera footage to back those claims. After phone calls with Maine’s senators, including Susan Collins, the secretary ordered an emergency halt to most traffic stops. But the decision surprised many in the White House, including President Trump, who reversed the order in a social-media post the next morning. Mullin wasn’t fired, but his authority was undercut, to the delight of the online MAGA mob that had been hammering the former Oklahoma senator as a wuss. Mullin declared on social media that he and Trump were “on the same page” on ICE enforcement tactics and priorities. But for a secretary whose views on the department he leads are still vague, the phrasing posed a question: What page are they on, exactly? Trump seeks the removal of millions of immigrants from U.S. cities and communities, and Mullin was brought in to do that more quietly and cheerfully than his predecessor. Kristi Noem was ousted after the killings in January of two U.S. citizens protesting ICE in Minneapolis. But Mullin, who started in March, is already being squeezed by the same forces that led to her removal. Trump’s supporters want to see the record-breaking deportation campaign that Trump promised them. But when DHS dials up the intensity of ICE enforcement, the broader public recoils from the violence it generates. Mullin, who dropped out of college to run his father’s plumbing business in eastern Oklahoma, took an unusual path to leading the most important domestic-policy initiative of Trump’s second term. He came to the job as somewhat of a blank slate, lacking both the ideological zeal of Stephen Miller, Trump’s immigration-policy chief, and the icy resolve of Noem, who pressed Trump’s mass-deportation campaign with a ruthless determination. Mullin was an improbable choice in other ways. He is not from a border state and did not arrive with deep knowledge of immigration policy or a background in counterterrorism. He never served in the military or in law enforcement, and he didn’t work on the congressional committees that directly oversee DHS and immigration. A former wrestler and mixed-martial-arts fighter, Mullin was known for organizing vigorous bipartisan workout sessions on the Hill, where he served for a decade in the House and for three years in the Senate. What was never in doubt were Mullin’s qualifications as a combatant for Trump. The president liked that Mullin was someone, he said, who “truly gets along well with people” but is also a “MAGA Warrior,” ready to brawl in congressional hearings and cable-news hits on the president’s behalf. Mullin spent his career running businesses he owns, rarely having to follow a chain of command. Some of the DHS officials I’ve spoken with, who are generally supportive of Mullin, say that he’s not used to getting permission from superiors and lacks the experience on immigration issues to understand the implications of policy decisions such as halting vehicle stops. “He wasn’t brought in because of his command of CBP and ICE issues,” one DHS official told me. “He was brought in to restore normalcy.” But normalcy is not what MAGA hard-liners want to see. When Mullin demonstrates the slightest unease with the human cost of enforcement operations, right-wing critics pounce, taunting him as “Markwayne Muslim” and the “Kung Fu Plumber.” Mullin does bring to DHS the one quality valued above any other in this administration: an imperturbable loyalty to Trump. The question hanging over him, as the pressure to ramp up deportations collides with the messy realities of carrying them out, is whether that loyalty has any limit. C reated in the aftermath of the September 11 attack, the Department of Homeland Security is a buttoned-up place with leaders who generally try to project steady resolve. Mullin brings more emotion to the job. Despite his tough-guy image, he struggles to hold back tears when he talks about his family. He got choked up during his confirmation hearing in March talking about his father’s work ethic and the devotion of his wife, Christie, whom he “fell in love with in third grade.” His eyes welled up during a budget hearing last month when he talked about sex-trafficking victims and confronting “evil in the world.” And he breaks down when talking about the brain injury his son survived in 2020, a story that also explains his personal devotion to Trump. Mullin’s son Jim was 15 years old when he took a blow to the head during a wrestling match and collapsed. The boy’s pulse dropped, and his lips turned blue. When Jim woke up in the hospital 26 hours later, he didn’t recognize his parents and had the motor skills of a toddler. “He’s a totally different kid,” Mullin later recalled. “We almost lost him.” But when the teenager was asked who the president was, his father says, he knew. Trump was delighted when he heard this. The president offered his plane to fly Jim to a rehab clinic in California and showed deep concern for the boy’s condition, visiting him and calling every week to check on his “buddy” for a year and a half. It was Trump’s affection, Mullin said, that helped Jim reclaim his memories and stay motivated. “We were acquaintances before that,” Mullin told a Senate panel earlier this year. “We’ve been friends ever since.” Mullin declared that he would follow Trump “into any situation”—and he has. Not long after Jim’s injury, Mullin joined the president in falsely claiming that the 2020 presidential election had been stolen, a position he stuck to even after he directly confronted rioters as they tried to break into the House chamber on January 6 . As a senator, Mullin championed the president’s massive spending bill last year, even though he’d first run for office as a fiscal hawk. And in March, when Trump asked Mullin to replace the scandal-ridden Noem at DHS, Mullin gave up what could have been a long congressional career for a job often described as one of the worst in Washington: a political hot seat with grave security responsibilities, atop a bureaucratic mash-up of 22 agencies and subagencies, and 275,000 employees. Mullin, who said he was surprised by Trump’s request, didn’t think twice.“It’s hard to say no to someone you have that kind of connection with,” Jason Owens, a former Border Patrol chief who is also Mullin’s cousin, told me. Jim Watson / AFP / Getty President Trump shakes hands with the newly sworn-in secretary of homeland security on March 24, 2026. The department has become far more partisan under Trump, and Mullin has done nothing to indicate that he’s likely to impose boundaries. Two days after the public reversal on vehicle stops, Mullin held a media briefing next door to the White House to push Trump’s favorite issue, echoing thin claims about election fraud while issuing threats—idle ones, legal experts say—to state officials who refuse to hand over voter data. When a reporter asked Mullin whether Trump had known in advance about the order halting ICE vehicle stops, Mullin dissembled, insisting that there was “no daylight” between them. “I’ve turned down the temperature of the stove, to try to get it correct, without boiling over,” Mullin said, touting record numbers of immigration arrests in recent weeks. Asked to clarify what he meant by “temperature,” Mullin said that he wanted less intense media coverage, not less aggressive ICE operations. “We’re turning up the heat on the streets,” he said. Mullin is 48, compact and powerful, with a thick neck and tight-fitting suits that look as if they’re about to burst. His front teeth are fake, he says; the original ones were knocked out at a rodeo by a “horn to the mouth.” Bull riding, wrestling, and MMA fighting all involve short, performative bursts of action, not unlike partisan combat. Mullin shares his boss’s urge to lunge at any slight but also to befriend former enemies if they decide to embrace him. Sean O’Brien—the burly Teamsters boss whom Mullin told to “stand your butt up” and fight him during a 2023 Senate hearing—ended up endorsing Mullin’s nomination to lead DHS after the president encouraged the two to make peace. During the confirmation hearing, O’Brien sat just over his shoulder, next to Mullin’s wife, Christie. Miller remains the driving force behind Trump’s immigration agenda, even though his influence over day-to-day DHS operations has diminished somewhat since the Minneapolis shootings. Mullin, like Miller, delights in hyperbolic attacks on Democrats, but the two men are in many ways stylistic opposites: Mullin is earnest and unpretentious; emotional, not cerebral. It’s almost impossible to imagine Miller crying in public. [ Read: Kristi Noem is gone. Now mass deportations can really begin. ] Mullin has been a vocal supporter of ICE officers and Trump’s border wall, but he has also spoken sympathetically about hard-working immigrants who stay out of trouble, sounding more like a conventional pro-business Republican than a closed-borders ideologue. Some Republicans facing difficult midterm races hoped that Mullin would defuse some of the backlash to Trump’s heavy-handed tactics, while hard-liners have been wary of Mullin’s statements in support of legal immigration (they want none) and his emphasis on deporting dangerous criminals (they want more deportations than that). Others have even loftier dreams for Mullin. I was surprised when a conservative former DHS official told me that he expected Trump to seek a major immigration compromise before the end of his term, as a legacy-building project and a bid to bring Latino voters back to the Republican Party. Mullin, who has impeccable MAGA credentials and improbably friendly relations with key Democrats, would be the ideal person to make such a deal, he told me. Mullin’s desire to keep DHS out of the headlines was wishful thinking, if not naive. After a few weeks on the job, he threatened to curb international flights to U.S. airports that serve “sanctuary” jurisdictions that won’t help ICE, then backed off that idea after it was panned by business groups, members of both parties, and Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy. Although there are no longer sweeping crackdowns on major cities for the time being, ICE arrests are ramping up again to roughly 2,000 a day nationwide, nearly double the pace since spring. Andres Kudacki / Getty ICE agents arrive to clear protesters from a gate outside the Delaney Hall Detention Facility in Newark, New Jersey. Mullin has been trying to turn temperatures down in other ways. He has eased off warrantless searches and enforcement around sensitive locations such as courthouses and schools. ICE recruits are no longer being fast-tracked through the training academy, and Mullin has halted plans to house tens of thousands of detainees in retrofitted warehouses . But Mullin gets prickly when Democrats claim a victory that could make him look weak. When New Jersey Governor Mikie Sherrill, a Democrat, boasted on social media last month that DHS had “backed down” from its plan to open an ICE jail in her state, Mullin reversed course. “DHS will NEVER back down,” he declared. “We will be keeping this site for a detention center.” M ullin says that his personal mantra is “Love the people, love the call,” and he has applied that as a management approach at DHS. Employee morale suffered during Noem’s tenure, and Mullin has devoted time to rallying the workforce, earning the nickname “MAGA Mayorkas.” It’s meant as a favorable comparison to Joe Biden’s DHS secretary, Alejandro Mayorkas, who was known within DHS for being kind to his staff even though Republicans treated him like the boogeyman. In Mullin’s office at DHS headquarters, located in the former Saint Elizabeths psychiatric-hospital complex in Southeast Washington, the secretary carries around a little squishy pink ball. Since middle school, he has used it as a stress-relief device and fidget tool—he calls it his “pacifier”—and he’ll bounce it to center himself when he wants to make a point. He grew up with a “bad speech impediment,” he says, and the alphabet soup of agencies and subagencies at DHS tripped him up at first. Mullin has since learned to carefully enunciate initialisms such as “CBP” in the correct order but has a curious habit of referring to DHS simply as “Homeland,” like the TV show, and his role as the “secretary of Homeland.” It makes the job sound warmer—like heartland —while also subtly reinforcing the view, popular with Trump appointees at DHS, that the nation’s top immigration-enforcement official should be a kind of cultural and demographic policeman as well as a real one. Michael M. Santiago / Getty Mullin speaks during a July press conference in New York City. [ Read: ‘Maybe DHS was a bad idea’ ] Mullin is only the second Native American to lead a Cabinet agency, but for much of his political career, he treated his Cherokee identity as more of a biographical footnote than a central feature. “I never knew I was special for being Cherokee until I came to D.C.,” Mullin said during a Senate hearing last year. “Where I’m from, everybody’s Indian, or wants to be.” When Democrats pressed Mullin last month during a DHS budget hearing to address questions about discrimination and racial profiling by immigration-enforcement officers, Mullin used his Cherokee identity as a shield. Representative Lou Correa of California, a Democrat, asked Mullin whether his constituents needed to carry their U.S. passport to protect themselves from ICE. “I’m from Indian Country, and we got a lot of brown people around there too,” Mullin fired back. After Representative Al Green of Texas, another Democrat, accused Mullin of defending racism, Mullin interrupted: “Are you calling me racist?” Green, incensed, shouted at him to “shut up,” but Mullin kept on swinging. “No one will call me a racist. I’m Cherokee,” he said. Mullin flies home to his family in Oklahoma most weekends on the same executive jet that Noem controversially purchased for the department, one of several missteps that led to her ouster. DHS told me that Mullin pays for his trips, but the department did not respond to follow-up questions about how his share of the cost is calculated. The Mullins—who married when they were teenagers—live in a large house in the Tulsa suburb of Bixby, where they moved a few years ago when their kids’ beloved wrestling coach took a job there. Mullin’s convoy of black SUVs has been spotted rolling around Bixby when he’s home. The couple have six children, three of whom are adopted. “I am proud to be pro-life,” Mullin has said of his large family. I went to northeast Oklahoma, where Mullin grew up, to gain a better understanding of his origins and his divided life between home and Washington, D.C. Although much of the state is flat, and rich in oil and gas, the 14 counties that compose the Cherokee Nation are a gentle landscape of forests, rivers, and cattle pastures in the foothills of the Ozark Mountains. Mullin was dragged on social media after asserting his Cherokee identity during the hearing, because he appears white. He is one of more than 460,000 enrolled members of the country’s largest federally recognized tribe. Eligibility is based on genealogical lineage, not on physical appearance or a minimum “blood quantum.” Members must trace direct lineage to a Cherokee ancestor inscribed in the Dawes Rolls, a federal registry of tribal members in Oklahoma kept from 1898 to 1914. Chuck Hoskin Jr., the chief of the Cherokee Nation, told me that he estimates that the majority of enrolled members have one-eighth or less Cherokee DNA. People who see Mullin “may say, ‘That doesn’t fit my perception of what a Native American should be,’” Hoskin said. “But it’s very much statistically who we are.” Other Cherokee members I met described their tribal heritage as Mullin does, as a source of pride but not necessarily the defining feature of their life. It’s also a source of social-welfare benefits. Cherokee identity has long been bound up in the tribe’s relationship to the federal government; enrollments surged during the creation of the Dawes Rolls, Julie L. Reed, a historian at the University of Tulsa, told me. In the mid-20th century, more white-passing members were eager to assimilate and downplay their heritage. Things have come full circle in recent decades, Reed said, the result of government benefits, federal-contracting advantages, and a resurgent pride in Cherokee identity. “People whose families had distanced themselves from the Cherokee Nation for all sorts of reasons over the course of the 20th century found their way back once the Nation really started to have real economic, political, and social impacts on the surrounding area,” Reed said. The Cherokee Nation, whose chief is an elected official, is an economic juggernaut with annual revenues of more than $3 billion from casinos, federal contracts, and an array of tribe-owned businesses. One of the region’s largest employers, the Nation has invested more than $1.2 billion into capital projects in recent years, according to Hoskin. Tahlequah, the Cherokee capital, is a thriving midsize town, with chain stores along the highways and a welcoming, well-maintained historic district. There’s a new medical complex, along with new community centers, senior care, playgrounds, and schools, some offering Cherokee-language immersion programs. Oklahoma was solidly Democratic for decades after it became the 46th state, in 1907, especially after the New Deal brought federal jobs and benefits to counties that were among the nation’s poorest. Benny Denton, an 85-year-old rancher who lives near the Mullin-family homestead, told me that when he was growing up, “I didn’t know of anybody who actually starved to death. But there wasn’t a rabbit in the field or a fish in the river.” The Cherokee Nation, whose government is nonpartisan, has spent the past several decades building the kind of cradle-to-grave welfare state once contemplated in the New Deal, even as Democrats have virtually disappeared from Oklahoma public office. T he ranch where Mullin grew up is less than a mile from the Arkansas state line, outside the tiny town of Westville, at the end of a narrow road winding past battered houses and trailers. A tall metal fence surrounds the 1,600-acre property, its gates engraved with the large M of the family logo. Mullin is the youngest of seven children, and friends and neighbors of the family spoke highly of his parents as hardworking, devout, and down-to-earth. Mullin’s father has a reputation for being somewhat stern, and he disciplined his kids with a belt; his mother is known for being warm, chatty, and a prodigious baker of cookies. Mullin was named after her brothers, Mark and Wayne. John Garrett, the 84-year-old chief justice of the Cherokee Nation Supreme Court, has known the family for decades and remembers Mullin accompanying his father to plumbing calls as a teenager. He was ambitious, a “go-getter,” Garrett told me. “He just had a lot of energy.” Tom Williams / CQ Roll Call / Getty Mullin, then a member of the House, gives a tour of the Capitol Rotunda to friends in 2016. [ Read: High stakes, and a low bar, for Markwayne Mullin at DHS ] The state’s shift to the Republican Party happened during those teen years, and the party’s attacks on “big government” found traction in a region with a fraught relationship to federal agencies that many Cherokee both depended on and resented. Mullin’s father embodied the change. He was a small-business owner who didn’t hide his frustrations with the IRS. Mullin was a devoted son who ran errands for his father. “You wouldn’t want to get in his father’s way,” said Denton, who owned the hardware store in town. Mullin enrolled in college on a wrestling scholarship but left to help run the family plumbing business. Over the years, he grew the company—known for its signature red vans and its slogan, “The Red Rooter”—and became its pitchman in commercials. Working around construction sites in the Tulsa metro area was probably when Mullin first had experience with immigrant laborers, people I spoke with there told me; eastern Oklahoma was not a destination for immigrants when Mullin was growing up. Records show that Mullin Plumbing has earned more than $520,000 in federal contracts since 2010, mostly through the Indian Health Service. Mullin Plumbing was listed as minority-owned, Native American–owned, American Indian–owned, and a “small disadvantaged business,” but according to contracting records, those categories were not a factor in the awards. In 2011, before he entered politics, Mullin began hosting a weekly radio show, House Talk , modeled in part after NPR’s Car Talk , to dispense advice on home improvement and repairs. Friends of the family told me that they think Mullin’s father pushed him to run for Congress around the same time to deploy his combativeness against environmental regulators and tax collectors. Mullin railed against the Affordable Care Act, opposing mandates that would require him to offer coverage to his employees despite having access himself to free health care as a tribal member. Mullin’s 2012 campaign slogan was “A rancher. A businessman. Not a politician.” When he arrived in Washington, he said , he didn’t know how to put on a tie. Mullin positioned himself firmly among the most conservative members of Congress, proposing, for example, a total abortion ban , even when a mother’s life is in danger. When he first took office, he pledged to serve a maximum of three terms, then broke the promise in 2018. Mullin explained the decision in a video , seated with Christie, saying that he “didn’t understand politics” when he got to Washington. With Trump in office, Mullin said, he could have a bigger impact if he stayed on the job. It turned out to be the most pivotal moment of his career, consolidating his transition from D.C. outsider to MAGA insider. “The first lesson I learned up there is, you gotta build relationships,” Mullin said. A private-equity company acquired Mullin Plumbing in 2021 for an undisclosed sum, estimated in the tens of millions of dollars’ worth of shares on the same day of the sale. The next year, Mullin won a special election for an open Senate seat. In both chambers, he was one of the few lawmakers without a four-year college degree, but his recent financial disclosures show an estimated net worth somewhere from $29 million to $97 million. John Bankel, a GOP activist in the area who knows the family, told me that he doubts that DHS will be Mullin’s last job in politics. Christie Mullin believes deeply in her husband’s political talents and “is the one who pushes Markwayne to the next level,” he said. Bankel told me that he thinks Mullin could run for president someday. Anna Moneymaker / Getty Mullin walks through the Capitol in 2025. Friends of the family told me that Mullin has sometimes regretted not serving in the military or law enforcement. The DHS-secretary job gives him a chance to be a man of action, with a lead role in national security and law enforcement. Mullin’s grandfather, who was Cherokee and a competitive bull rider, served in World War II, and last year, Mullin cited his role in liberating Nazi concentration camps when condemning a group of young Republicans who’d made racist and anti-Semitic statements in group chats. Mullin has tried to burnish his own reputation for daring, though in a cryptic way. He has told interviewers that he performed “special assignments” outside of the U.S. military. After the Taliban took Kabul in 2021, Mullin asked the U.S. ambassador to Tajikistan to help him bring in a large amount of cash so he could rent a helicopter and lead a dangerous mission into Afghan territory to rescue a mother and her four children, all U.S. citizens. Mullin threatened U.S.-embassy staff when they turned him down. After Trump nominated him for the DHS job, Mullin told senators that the “smell” of war was something he would “never forget.” When Democrats asked what he meant, Mullin said that he’d taken a classified trip in 2016 as a member of Congress that required specialized conflict-zone training. But he refused to provide more details. “There’s another side of my bio that I don’t talk about, nor will I,” Mullin previously said on a podcast , in no great hurry to dispel the intrigue. Soon, it came out that Mullin had told Senate colleagues during a 2023 closed-door meeting with Jordan’s King Abdullah II that he and other Americans were once rescued when the king sent his personal helicopter, because they could not get help from the U.S. military, according to The New York Times . Mullin described blood all over the helicopter. Had Mullin made a top-secret trip into war-torn Syria? Mullin has refused to say, and no one from the administration has cleared up the mystery. N ot far from Mullin’s old high school, in Stilwell, is a cemetery with a historic marker that commemorates the end of the Trail of Tears, the 1,000-mile journey made by thousands of Cherokee who were forced out of their ancestral homes in southeastern Appalachia under the Indian Removal Act of 1830. The ordeal remains central to Cherokee identity—it’s the foundational story of the Cherokee Nation as well as its greatest tragedy. From 1838 to 1839, more than 3,500 Cherokee arrived at the site in Stilwell, an official “disbandment depot” just outside the borders of the settled United States. The deported families waited in lines to receive government provisions and were sent off into the forest to rebuild their lives. Most of them spent the winter in tents around the edges of the depot site. Some of the more than 1,000 Cherokee who died making the journey are buried in the Stilwell cemetery, according to the National Park Service. Removal remains an official government term, though today it’s used primarily at DHS to tally ICE deportations. There is no comparison, of course, between the removal of immigrants who enter the United States illegally and the relocation of the Cherokee and other tribes. That doesn’t make deportation less fraught or painful for those who develop deep ties to the United States or have children here. Taking people from their home and separating families are grave and consequential acts. Mullin so far has engaged little with these messy realities, at least publicly. Mostly, he has echoed the rhetoric of Trump officials who prefer to depict ICE enforcement as a matter of public safety focused on the removal of dangerous criminals. (ICE data show that only a small percentage of deportees—ranging from about 5 to 15 percent—have violent felony convictions.) Like Tom Homan, the White House “border czar,” Mullin has seemed most animated when talking about the trafficking and abuse of underage migrants who arrived in record numbers during the Biden administration, an issue that lacks the moral complexity of much of ICE’s other work. Republican lawmakers used the reconciliation process last month to score $70 billion in additional funding for Trump’s immigration crackdown over the next three years, adding to the $170 billion they’d obtained in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act last summer without a single Democratic vote. Trump has nominated a Mullin loyalist, the former Oklahoma state trooper Lance Schroyer, to be the next ICE director. The flood of congressional funding and recent Supreme Court rulings in favor of tougher enforcement have positioned Trump’s mass-deportation plan for takeoff . That leaves Mullin, who launched his political career attacking big government, as the face of one of the largest-ever law-enforcement operations in U.S. history. Mullin’s “Homeland” department will soon have twice as many ICE officers on the streets, and all of the money it needs to round up and deport 1 million people a year. Mullin would like that to happen quietly, but it will likely trigger new pushback and protest, testing just how far he’s willing to go for Trump. Marie-Rose Sheinerman and Isabel Ruehl contributed to this report. *Illustration sources: Jim Watson / Getty; Andrew Lichtenstein / Corbis / Getty; Elizabeth Fernandez / Getty; Chip Somodevilla / Getty.

items.title: 𝐒𝐨𝐮𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐭𝐞́ 𝐞́𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐠𝐞́𝐭𝐢𝐪𝐮𝐞 : 𝐥𝐞 𝐏𝐫𝐞𝐦𝐢𝐞𝐫 𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐞 𝐢𝐧𝐚𝐮𝐠𝐮𝐫𝐞 𝐥𝐚 𝐜𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐥𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐢𝐪𝐮𝐞 𝐝𝐞 𝐏𝐚𝐛𝐫𝐞́
items.description: Le Camarade Premier ministre, Chef du Gouvernement, Rimtalba Jean Emmanuel Ouédraogo, a procédé, ce mardi 21 juillet 2026 à Pabré, à l’inauguration officielle de la centrale thermique d’urgence d’une capacité de 50 mégawatts. ‎ ‎Réalisée en seulement 90 jours, contre un délai contractuel de 120 jours, cette infrastructure, d’un coût de 18 milliards de FCFA, vient renforcer les capacités de production de la Société nationale d’électricité du Burkina (SONABEL) et contribuer à l’amélioration de l’offre énergétique nationale. ‎ ‎La cérémonie est intervenue une heure après la pose de la première pierre de la future centrale thermique de Ouaga Sud-Est, dans la commune de Saaba, par le Camarade ministre de l’Énergie, des Mines et des Carrières, Yacouba Zabré Gouba. D’une capacité projetée de 120 mégawatts, cette nouvelle infrastructure nécessitera un investissement estimé à 133 milliards de FCFA. ‎ ‎Le Premier ministre a salué la mise en service de la centrale de Pabré, premier jalon d’un vaste programme engagé dans le cadre de la Révolution progressiste populaire, en vue d’assurer l’autonomie stratégique et la souveraineté énergétique du Burkina Faso. « Aujourd’hui, la souveraineté du Burkina Faso est également en marche dans le domaine de l’énergie. Nous n’aurons de repos que lorsque nous aurons réussi à faire en sorte que la production nationale d’électricité soit supérieure à la demande », a-t-il soutenu. ‎ ‎Le Chef du Gouvernement a relevé que les choix opérés par le passé, principalement fondés sur l’importation de l’électricité, ont montré leurs limites, en exposant le pays aux aléas extérieurs et aux perturbations de l’approvisionnement énergétique. ‎« Notre pays avait fait le choix de mettre l’accent sur l’importation de l’énergie. Cette option a montré ses limites, parce que nous restions tributaires de la qualité de la production des pays d’où nous importions l’électricité. Aujourd’hui, nous avons décidé de construire nos propres capacités de production pour aller vers l’autonomie stratégique et la souveraineté énergétique », a-t-il déclaré. ‎ ‎Dans son discours, le ministre de l’Énergie, des Mines et des Carrières, le Camarade Yacouba Zabré Gouba, a souligné que la mise en service de cette centrale va bien au-delà d’un simple investissement dans le secteur de l’énergie. ‎ ‎Selon le ministre, cette réalisation traduit la volonté du Gouvernement de bâtir progressivement l’indépendance énergétique du pays, conformément à la vision portée par le Président du Faso, Chef de l’État, le Camarade Capitaine Ibrahim Traoré. Cette ambition répond à un impératif de souveraineté nationale, de développement économique et d’amélioration des conditions de vie des populations. ‎ ‎Au nom de l’entreprise Al Ahram Taqa Burkina, son Administrateur général, Massoud Hassan Abdo Jaied, a exprimé sa gratitude aux autorités burkinabè, à la SONABEL ainsi qu’à l’ensemble des partenaires ayant contribué à la réalisation du projet. ‎ ‎Il a réaffirmé l’engagement de son groupe à accompagner durablement le Burkina Faso dans sa marche vers la souveraineté énergétique, rappelant que l’électricité constitue un levier essentiel pour le développement de l’industrie, de l’agriculture, des mines, de l’éducation et des services publics. ‎ ‎Au cours de la cérémonie, le Président-directeur général et l’Administrateur général de l’entreprise Al Ahram Taqa ont été élevés au grade de Chevalier de l’Ordre du mérite burkinabè, en reconnaissance de leur contribution au développement du secteur énergétique national. ‎ ‎La centrale thermique de Pabré s’inscrit dans un vaste programme national qui prévoit prochainement l’inauguration de nouvelles centrales à Bobo-Dioulasso, Koudougou, Kaya, Komsilga et Toécé, pour une capacité additionnelle globale de plus de 220 mégawatts. ‎ ‎À travers ces investissements, le Gouvernement entend accroître durablement les capacités nationales de production d’électricité, répondre à la demande croissante en énergie et soutenir le développement économique et social du Burkina Faso. ‎ ‎𝐃𝐂𝐑𝐏/𝐏𝐫𝐢𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞

items.title: PML-N supremo Nawaz kicks off party's election campaign in AJK
items.description: The PML-N on Tuesday formally kicked off its election campaign in Azad Jammu and Kashmir (AJK), with party supremo Nawaz Sharif addressing supporters in the regional capital. Elections in AJK, previously set for July 27, are now to be held in three division-wise stages , beginning on July 27 and concluding on August 10. Flanked by Punjab Chief Minister Maryam Nawaz, the ex-premier began his address by noting that he was among the people of Muzaffarabad after several years. He expressed his affection for the people of Kashmir and highlighted the absence of a motorway between Kohala and Muzaffarabad. “A most beautiful motorway should be constructed along the Jhelum River from Mansehra to Muzaffarabad, and from Muzaffarabad to Mirpur,” the PML-N supremo said. He recalled that he had approved a plan for the region’s development when his party’s Raja Farooq Haider was the AJK PM in his last tenure. Nawaz said that he had never differentiated between AJK and the provinces of Pakistan. “AJK is as dear to me as Punjab is. It is my second home.” Reminiscent of his older public addresses, the PML-N president said, “I know you love me. I love you too. The people of Muzaffarabad always want to hear this from me.” Asserting that Muzaffarabad should be made into the most beautiful city of Pakistan, Nawaz said he told Maryam that the steps she is taking for Punjab as the chief minister should be replicated in AJK. He reiterated that whatever CM Maryam was doing in Lahore and other cities of Punjab should also be “copied” in Muzaffarabad and other cities of AJK. “I told Maryam that you are coming here as a guest goes to a host’s home with fruits and sweets, so present the same to the people of Muzaffarabad,” he said, adding that the PML-N’s manifesto was wide-ranging. Nawaz promised that every development project would be implemented and that the PML-N would resolve “all the issues” if it wins the elections. He cautioned his party leadership in AJK that he would visit AJK after every three months to see whether the party’s manifesto was being implemented or not. “If we win the election, I will come after every three months, and will visit each and every street with the prime minister.” Nawaz highlighted that when he was the prime minister of Pakistan, he had doubled the budget of AJK. “We will not double it this time; we will increase this by 10 times,” he declared. For Muzaffarabad, he announced that at least 10 green buses would arrive in AJK within the next few days. “You will see electric buses running on Muzaffarabad’s roads in the upcoming days.” “We are also bringing 12 hospitals on wheels/mobile hospitals here within a few days,” he added, while regretting that people of AJK did not have adequate health and emergency facilities. He also criticised the governments that ruled AJK after PML-N, claiming that they did not do anything other than “sloganeering and inciting people’s emotions”. The PML-N has announced its candidates for 37 of the 45 constituencies of the AJK Legislative Assembly. ‘Never-ending relationship’ In her address at the gathering, CM Maryam said that the people of Kashmir were like a family to her. She remarked that Pakistan and Kashmir had a “never-ending” relationship. The Punjab CM also referred to the situation in the region ongoing for the past over a month: “If there was Nawaz Sharif’s government in AJK, it would not face these issues as it is facing right now.” She claimed that during the 2021 elections in AJK, the PML-N had won 17 seats and the PTI could get only three. However, she alleged, then-PM Imran Khan ordered the swap of the voting results, stealing the PML-N’s mandate. More to follow

items.title: Prévention des inondations à Thiès: des instructions données pour finaliser les travaux de curage
items.description: Les quartiers qui font souvent l’objet d’inondations à Thiès ont été visités, hier, lundi 20 juillet, par le ministre de l'Hydraulique et de l'Assainissement. Cheikh Tidiane Dièye souhaite que les travaux de curage soient bouclés avant la fin du mois. THIÈS – Le ministre de l'Hydraulique et de l'Assainissement, Cheikh Tidiane Dièye, a effectué, hier, lundi 20 juillet, une visite dans les points inondables de la ville de Thiès. Il s’agit de Dalot de Sampathé, les points bas de Mbamba et le canal à ciel ouvert de Nguinth. M. Dièye en a profité pour évaluer les infrastructures hydrauliques. Il a donné des instructions pour la finalisation des chantiers, notamment ceux de la station de Mbambara, avant le début du mois d’août. Le ministre a magnifié la collaboration interinstitutionnelle impliquant les élus locaux et les services techniques pour libérer les passages d'eau et curer les canaux avant que l’hivernage ne s’installe définitivement. Il s'est « engagé à transformer les interventions d'urgence traditionnelles en solutions structurelles durables, promettant de mobiliser les ressources budgétaires nécessaires pour protéger définitivement la ville de Thiès contre les inondations ». Dans les points bas de Mbambara et Tokhalit, où l'Office national de l’assainissement (Onas) est en train de réaliser une station, Cheikh Tidiane Dièye a donné des instructions pour que les travaux soient bien réalisés. Concernant le dalot de Sampathé, il a demandé la mise sur pied d’un comité multi-acteurs, en plus des mesures d’urgence d'accompagnement et de mitigation. Pour le canal de Nguinth, le ministre a rappelé qu’il avait pris les devants en dépêchant sur les lieux le directeur de la Prévention des inondations. Ce dernier, informe-t-il, lui a d’ailleurs transmis son rapport. M. Dièye a demandé à ses services d'anticiper et d'agir rapidement pour le reprofilage du canal. Il a assuré que ce travail sera poursuivi pour faciliter l'écoulement des eaux, bloquées souvent par le typha, mais aussi dégager des habitations installées au milieu du tracé du canal. Le ministre a, en outre, instruit le gouverneur de région, en relation avec le maire de Thiès Nord, de prendre des mesures urgentes pour la démolition des habitations dont les propriétaires ont déjà été dédommagés. Le maire de la ville de Thiès, Dr Babacar Diop, a exprimé sa reconnaissance au ministre Cheikh Tidiane Dièye. Il a déclaré que, contrairement aux interventions passées, cette visite se distingue par l'élaboration d'un plan de suivi rigoureux destiné à transformer les promesses politiques en solutions concrètes et durables pour les populations sinistrées. M. Diop a noté une volonté politique nouvelle qui privilégie l'efficacité opérationnelle face aux défis climatiques saisonniers. Mbaye Sarr DIAKHATÉ (Correspondant)

items.title: Jamaat-i-Islami announces nationwide road blockade against petroleum levy
items.description: LAHORE: Jamaat-i-Islami Pakistan (JI) emir Hafiz Naeemur Rehman on Tuesday announced a nationwide highway blockade and sit-ins on Aug 7 as part of the second phase of the party’s anti-petroleum levy and anti-inflation campaign. The government had announced a petroleum and carbon levy on June 14, 2025, under an iron-clad commitment with the International Monetary Fund (IMF). The JI campaign demands the immediate abolition of the petroleum levy, cancellation of costly agreements with independent power producers (IPPs), and measures to curb inflation and power outages. Addressing a press conference at Mansoorah, Rehman said that JI workers and supporters would stage sit-ins on roads connecting all districts across the country, adding that only ambulances would be allowed to pass. To mobilise public support ahead of the protest, the JI would hold 20,000 nationwide corner meetings before Aug 7, he announced. The JI emir warned the government against using undemocratic tactics to suppress the protest movement, saying that any such action would turn the anti-inflation campaign into a movement aimed at bringing down the government. JI deputy secretaries Nazir Janjua, Waqas Jafri and Azhar Iqbal Hassan, Lahore emir Ziauddin Ansari, and party Information Secretary Shakil Ahmed Turabi were also present at the press conference. Rehman urged the government to immediately abolish the petroleum levy, scrap expensive IPP contracts and take effective measures to end electricity load-shedding. He also called on provincial governments to restore food prices to the levels prevailing before the Iran-US conflict. Criticising the government’s decision to allow the Oil and Gas Regulatory Authority (Ogra) to adjust petroleum prices on a daily basis, he termed the move deceptive, questioning what authority Ogra had over the petroleum levy and other taxes imposed on every litre of fuel. He said that the government was extorting the public through the petroleum levy, adding that if the levy and other taxes were abolished, the retail price of petrol would fall below Rs200 per litre. Instead of burdening citizens, he said, the rulers should “reduce their own privileges and extravagant expenditures”. Calling on the public to participate in the Aug 7 demonstrations, Rehman urged young people to bring their motorcycles onto the roads to strengthen the blockade. Turning to broader governance issues, the JI chief criticised the performance of provincial governments, saying poverty in Punjab had increased by 41 per cent over the past six years. He claimed Punjab Chief Minister Maryam Nawaz had “sold 11,000 schools” and purchased an aircraft worth Rs11 billion for herself. He also criticised lawmakers in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, saying that they had passed legislation granting themselves unlimited benefits. He described the PPP as the establishment’s “A-plus team”, asking its chairman Bilawal Bhutto-Zardari to tell the people who was in government in Azad Jammu and Kashmir (AJK) before issuing statements and deceiving the public. Expressing concern over the deteriorating security situation in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, particularly its southern districts, and Balochistan, Rehman urged the government to pursue dialogue to resolve ongoing challenges. He said the JI was engaging various stakeholders on the Balochistan issue and hoped that a grand jirga would be convened soon. He welcomed reports of negotiations between state institutions and the Joint Action Committee in Azad Kashmir, saying that the JI had consistently advocated dialogue to resolve the crisis and had itself made efforts to facilitate talks. However, he said the government had not responded to the party’s initiatives. Rehman said the second phase of the JI’s protest campaign had been launched after internal consultations and would continue until the public’s demands were met. He added that the party would engage religious scholars, traders, lawyers, students, civil society and other segments of society to broaden support for the movement. Rehman has repeatedly criticised the government for its major hikes in petroleum prices — following the closure of the Hormuz Strait — and termed them a burden on the common citizen. Earlier this month, JI activists staged demonstrations in KP against the high prices of petroleum products and rising inflation in the country. This followed rallies across the province last month against the hike in prices of electricity, gas and petroleum products, as well as heavy taxes imposed in the budget. In May, Rehman approached the Federal Constitutional Court (FCC) against the petroleum levy, arguing that the government had effectively granted itself unrestricted power to tax citizens through executive notifications without parliamentary oversight.

Burkina: obligation aux admis aux BAC C, D, E, F et H de vérifier leur admissibilité pour l’Académie technologique du Faso
items.description: Le Ministre, Directeur de Cabinet Martha Céleste Anderson D. Medah invite, dans un communiqué ce mardi 21 juillet 2026, les admis aux baccalauréats session 2026 dans les séries C, D, E, F et H à se connecter à la plateforme d'admission accessible à l'adresse recrutement.atf.gov.bf afin de vérifier leur admissibilité pour l’ Académie technologique du Faso (ATF) et de marquer leurs choix de filières. «Cette démarche est obligatoire pour chaque lauréat, qu'il envisage ou non de poursuivre son parcours à l'ATF», précise le communiqué. COMMUNIQUÉ Créée sous l'autorité du Camarade Capitaine Ibrahim TRAORÉ, Président du Faso, Chef de l'État, l'Académie Technologique du Faso (ATF) porte l'ambition de former, sur le sol national, les ingénieurs et les bâtisseurs qui conduiront la souveraineté scientifique, technologique et industrielle du Burkina Faso. Elle incarne la conviction que la reconquête de notre destin passe par la maîtrise, par nos propres enfants, des sciences et des technologies qui façonnent le monde. Pour que cette ambition se traduise en excellence, l'État mobilise des moyens exceptionnels. Les élèves ingénieurs de l'ATF bénéficieront d'infrastructures modernes, d'équipements scientifiques de pointe, d'un encadrement académique de très haut niveau et d'un régime d'internat assurant des conditions d'étude, de discipline et d'épanouissement parmi les meilleures du continent. La Nation investit dans ses talents afin que rien ne manque à ceux qui auront l'honneur d'y être admis. À cet effet, le Directeur de Cabinet du Président du Faso porte à la connaissance de l'ensemble des lauréats du Baccalauréat, session de 2026, que la période d'admission à l'Académie Technologique du Faso est ouverte du mardi 21 au vendredi 25 juillet 2026. Durant cette période, tous les lauréats du BAC 2026 dans les scientifiques et techniques (C, D, E, F, H), sans exception, sont tenus de se connecter à la plateforme d'admission accessible à l'adresse recrutement.atf.gov.bf afin de vérifier leur admissibilité et de marquer leurs choix de filières. Cette démarche est obligatoire pour chaque lauréat, qu'il envisage ou non de poursuivre son parcours à l'ATF. L'admission à l'Académie Technologique du Faso demeure un choix libre. Elle est aussi un privilège réservé aux plus méritants. La Nation attend de ses meilleurs bacheliers qu'ils répondent présents à cet appel de l'excellence et qu'ils mettent leur talent au service de la construction du Faso. Ceux qui feront ce choix rejoindront une institution d'élite dont les diplômés seront aux avant-postes des grands chantiers de transformation du pays. Le Directeur de Cabinet du Président du Faso invite les parents, les tuteurs et l'ensemble de la communauté éducative à accompagner les lauréats dans l'accomplissement de cette formalité dans les délais impartis. Ouagadougou, le 21 juillet 2026 Le Camarade Ministre, Directeur de Cabinet Martha Céleste Anderson D. MEDAH L’article Burkina: obligation aux admis aux BAC C, D, E, F et H de vérifier leur admissibilité pour l'Académie technologique du Faso est apparu en premier sur Wakat Séra .

items.title: Ismaïla Madior Fall ouvre le bal des procès des anciens ministres
items.description: L'ancien ministre de la Justice sous le régime du Président Macky Sall, Ismaïla Madior Fall, comparaît, aujourd'hui, devant la Haute Cour de justice. Il est le premier ex-membre du gouvernement à être jugé par cette juridiction dans le cadre des dossiers instruits à la suite de la deuxième alternance. Inculpé pour tentative de corruption, Ismaïla Madior Fall a été placé sous assignation à résidence sous surveillance électronique à l'issue de son audition devant la Commission d'instruction de la Haute Cour de justice. Cette affaire est liée à une parcelle de 9.598 m² située à Guédiawaye, initialement affectée au ministère de la Justice pour la construction d'un vaste complexe judiciaire. Le projet devait comprendre un Palais de justice, un centre de casiers judiciaires, un centre médico-social ainsi qu'un centre de surveillance pour bracelets électroniques. Selon les éléments du dossier, le protocole d'accord prévoyait que les travaux soient financés sur fonds propres par l'entrepreneur Cheikh Guèye. En contrepartie, une portion de l'assiette foncière devait lui être transférée en guise de paiement. Cheikh Guèye accuse, toutefois, l'ancien ministre d'avoir exigé un pot-de-vin de 250 millions de FCfa en échange de la signature dudit protocole. Il affirme avoir remis une première somme de 50 millions de FCfa en espèces dans le bureau d'Ismaïla Madior Fall, en présence de Mouhamed Anas El Bachir Wane. Dans la même affaire, ce dernier et Cheikh Guèye sont poursuivis pour escroquerie sur les deniers publics, corruption, association de malfaiteurs et blanchiment de capitaux. A. DIOUF

items.title: Diamond smile: Singapore jails Indian man for gem in mouth theft
items.description: A Singapore court sentenced an Indian tourist to more than two years in jail over a failed bid to steal a diamond by hiding it in his accomplice’s mouth. Mangroliya Manojkumar Kurjibhai, 41, and his accomplice visited a jewellery store in Singapore’s Chinatown on June 19 with their eyes on a 4.95-carat diamond worth $154,000, according to court documents seen by AFP on Tuesday. Mangroliya distracted the shop manager while his partner, 30-year-old Serasiya Milan Ramnikbhai, allegedly swapped the diamond with a fake hidden in his mouth, local media reported. The two men then left the store without buying anything, with Serasiya now carrying the real stone in his mouth. The theft was caught on surveillance camera, and the pair were arrested at the airport later that day – with the stolen gem recovered from one of their bags. Mangroliya was sentenced to two years, two months and two weeks in jail after pleading guilty last week, court papers said. Serasiya is in remand and scheduled for a hearing on Friday. The pair reportedly arranged for a counterfeit diamond — matching the real gem’s specifications and serial number — to be made in India before travelling to Singapore. It is unclear how they obtained the diamond’s details in advance. Earlier this month, jewellery worth millions of euros was stolen in a burglary at the museum of French luxury glassmaker Lalique, the company and an investigation source said. The thief or thieves broke into the museum in Wingen-sur-Moder in northeastern France around 5:30am (8:30am PKT) and headed straight for the jewellery room, the source close to the investigation said. “Around twenty pieces of jewellery were stolen. The loss is currently being assessed but could amount to several million euros, likely close to four million,” the source said. The museum said on its website that it will be closed for several days because of the burglary. “An alarm went off, but by the time the security company had completed its checks, it was a cleaning lady who arrived first on the scene and called the police,” the investigation source added. The CCTV footage is currently being examined.

items.title: La faim dans le monde résiste, l'Afrique en première ligne
items.description: Quelque 645 millions de personnes ont souffert de la faim l'an dernier, soit 7,8% de la population mondiale, une légère amélioration qui risque de se heurter cette année aux chocs de la guerre au Moyen-Orient et du climat, estime mardi la FAO. La situation est particulièrement critique en Afrique, où 20% de la population est sujette à une sous-alimentation – c'est-à-dire un manque chronique de calories -, indique ce rapport annuel. Globalement, la crise économique post-Covid a plongé dans la faim des millions de personnes supplémentaires: 688 millions d'êtres d'humains étaient concernés en 2022. Depuis, la situation s'est redressée, sans retrouver toutefois les niveaux de 2019, selon l'Organisation pour l'alimentation et l'agriculture, agence de l'ONU. En 2030, l'ONU projette entre 510 et 520 millions de personnes sous-nourries, dont plus de la moitié en Afrique. « Le centre de gravité de la faim s'est déplacé de l'Asie jusqu'en Afrique », décrit à l'AFP David Laborde, directeur de la division économie agroalimentaire à la FAO. « Il y a 25 ans, la Chine avait encore un problème de faim, ils l'ont réglé; l'Inde a aussi un problème mais ils mettent beaucoup d'efforts pour le régler activement ». Les chocs climatiques de plus en plus sévères sont ressentis particulièrement durement en Afrique. « Des conflits comme le Soudan, ce qui se passe dans la région sahélienne, en République démocratique du Congo, pèsent aussi lourdement sur des gens qui ne peuvent avoir accès à leur terre ou n'ont plus de revenus », poursuit M. Laborde. Quant à la crise économique post-Covid, « on a commencé à récupérer, mais moins vite qu'on ne le pensait », dit-il. Selon ce rapport, 2,69 milliards de personnes – près d'une personne sur trois – ne peuvent s'offrir une alimentation saine, les coûts ayant crû de 25% en cinq ans. L'obésité chez les adultes continue elle de croître, à 16,2% de la population en 2024 (et 18,6% projetés en 2030). Cette année des événements, dont la guerre au Moyen-Orient, « menacent de saper les progrès » en Afrique et en Asie, alerte le rapport. La FAO se dit aussi « vigilante » quant à l'impact du phénomène océanique El Niño sur les récoltes, en Inde, en Afrique ou dans le « corridor sec » de l'Amérique du Sud, déjà très vulnérables face à l'insécurité alimentaire. The post La faim dans le monde résiste, l'Afrique en première ligne appeared first on Hespress Français - Actualités du Maroc .

items.title: Le HCR annonce la mort ou la disparition de 144 migrants au large de la Mauritanie
items.description: Quelque 144 réfugiés et migrants sont morts ou portés disparus au large des côtes mauritaniennes la semaine dernière, lors de tentatives de traversée irrégulière entre l’Afrique de l’Ouest et l'Europe, a indiqué mardi le Haut-Commissariat des Nations unies pour les réfugiés (HCR), qui déplore une nouvelle tragédie qui met en évidence le coût humain persistant de cette route migratoire parmi les plus meurtrières au monde. Selon le HCR, trois opérations de sauvetage et de débarquement menées entre les 14 et 18 juillet à Nouadhibou ont permis de secourir 387 personnes, l'Agence onusienne ayant participé à l'ensemble des opérations aux côtés des autorités mauritaniennes et de ses partenaires humanitaires afin d'assister les survivants et d'identifier les personnes vulnérables nécessitant une protection internationale. L'une des embarcations secourues le 18 juillet a connu un drame particulièrement meurtrier, avec 143 personnes présumées mortes ou disparues et seulement 38 survivants après près de 25 jours de dérive dans l'océan Atlantique à la suite de leur départ de Bufalo en Gambie, a précisé Matthew Saltmarsh, un porte-parole du HCR, lors d’un point de presse à Genève. Parmi les survivants figurent deux enfants ayant perdu tous les membres de leur famille durant la traversée et actuellement pris en charge dans un hôpital. Un autre décès a également été signalé parmi les passagers d'une embarcation débarquée le 14 juillet. Le HCR rappelle que la route atlantique reliant l'Afrique de l'Ouest aux îles Canaries demeure l'un des corridors migratoires mixtes les plus meurtriers au monde en raison des longues distances, des conditions océaniques imprévisibles, des embarcations précaires et surchargées ainsi que des difficultés d'accès à des opérations de secours rapides. L'Agence relève que, malgré une baisse de 61% des arrivées aux Canaries depuis le début de l'année par rapport à 2025, avec quelque 4.400 arrivées enregistrées au 15 juillet, les réfugiés et migrants continuent d'être exposés à des risques extrêmes, nombre d'entre eux entamant leur traversée à plus de 2.000 kms de leur destination. Depuis le début de l'année, plus de 40.000 arrivées par voie maritime ont été enregistrées en Europe sur les principales routes migratoires, contre 65.407 au cours du premier semestre de 2025. Le HCR appelle à redoubler d'efforts pour s'attaquer aux causes profondes des déplacements forcés et de la migration irrégulière, tout en renforçant les voies légales d'accès à la protection, notamment à travers l'augmentation des programmes de réinstallation, des possibilités de migration de travail et des opportunités d'éducation. Depuis le début de 2026, dix-sept opérations de débarquement ont été recensées à Nouadhibou et Nouakchott, permettant le sauvetage de 2.147 personnes. The post Le HCR annonce la mort ou la disparition de 144 migrants au large de la Mauritanie appeared first on Hespress Français - Actualités du Maroc .

items.title: Sudan’s Health Minister: Cholera outbreak in North Kordofan ‘contained’
items.description: Sudan’s Federal Minister of Health, Dr Haitham Mohamed Ibrahim, says cholera cases in the Mazroub area of North Kordofan have declined compared with recent weeks, while only a small number of cases have been recorded in Sheikan locality, with no evidence of wider transmission. Speaking to Radio Dabanga , Dr Ibrahim says Sudan declared itself free of cholera in January before new cases emerged in May in parts of West Kordofan under the control of the Rapid Support Forces (RSF). He says daily official reports submitted through the national disease surveillance system from all Sudanese states, including those under RSF control, prompted health authorities to collect and analyse samples, confirming cholera infections in a number of localities in West Kordofan. Dr Ibrahim says health officials responded immediately by directing organisations operating in the area, particularly the Kafa Organisation and Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF), to establish isolation centres in several West Kordofan localities. Daily surveillance and monitoring of cases were also introduced. He says population movements, particularly in war-affected areas, contributed to the spread of the disease after infected people travelled to Mazroub, where large numbers of cases were subsequently recorded. An isolation centre was established there, while international organisations and aid agencies provided medical supplies in coordination with North Kordofan state authorities, who also arranged for additional supplies to be delivered to the area. Dr Ibrahim says surveillance and daily monitoring continue, with the number of cholera cases in Mazroub now lower than in previous weeks. He adds that only limited cases have been reported in Sheikan locality and that there is no indication the disease is spreading there. Share article The post Sudan’s Health Minister: Cholera outbreak in North Kordofan ‘contained’ appeared first on Dabanga Radio TV Online .

items.title: Niger : arrivée d’un premier convoi algérien de 7 camions pour trois projets
items.description: La coopération entre l’Algérie et le Niger s’accélère et ne se limite pas au secteur de l’énergie. De nombreux projets de développement sont pris en charge dans ce pays du Sahel via l'Agence algérienne de coopération internationale, conformément à l’approche de l’Algérie, qui considère que la stabilité de la région passe par le développement économique et social. L'Agence algérienne de coopération prend en charge trois projets au Niger La télévision nigérienne a fait état, de l'arrivée dimanche 19 juillet, d’un premier convoi de sept gros camions d'Algérie transportant des équipements et du matériel de construction pour la réalisation d’infrastructures dans la région d’Agadez. Il s’agit d’un centre d'études islamiques, d’un centre d’hémodialyse et d’une polyclinique. Les trois projets seront réalisés par l’entreprise publique GCB (Génie Civil et Bâtiment), filiale de Sonatrach. “C’est avec une grande fierté que nous annonçons l’arrivée du convoi algérien pour le démarrage des travaux de réalisation des trois projets inscrits dans le cadre de la coopération algéro-nigérienne”, a déclaré sur place, à l’arrivée des sept camions, le directeur du projet au niveau de GCB, Mohamed Abidet. La coopération entre l'Algérie et le Niger va au-delà de l’énergie Ces projets entrent dans le cadre de la concrétisation des “importantes décisions stratégiques” prises lors de la visite du président Abdourahmane Tiani en Algérie en février dernier, a indiqué pour sa part le gouverneur d’Agadez Ibra Boulama Issa. Les premiers travaux ont déjà commencé, a souligné la télévision nigérienne. Après une crise qui a suivi le coup d'État à Niamey en juillet 2023, les relations entre les deux pays voisins sont revenues à la normale. L'Algérie et le Niger ont lancé cette année de grands projets énergétiques, dont le Gazoduc TSGP et la réalisation d’une centrale électrique de 40 mégawatts à Niamey, mise en chantier en mars dernier et inaugurée en juin en présence du Premier ministre Sifi Ghrieb. Les deux pays ont également convenu d'exploiter conjointement le champ pétrolier d’Afar, dans le nord du Niger. Un convoi de camions transportant des équipements et du matériel de construction en provenance d’Algérie est arrivé hier à Agadez. Pour constater de visu l’arrivée de ce premier convoi, le gouverneur de la région d’Agadez s’est rendu ce matin sur le site devant accueillir un… pic.twitter.com/atUn6pFkV4 — Radio Algeria international إذاعة الجزائر الدولية (@radioalginter) July 21, 2026 L’Algérie a aussi repris des relations normales avec le Mali après plusieurs années de crise, avec le retour cette semaine à leur poste des deux ambassadeurs respectifs et l’ouverture réciproque de l'espace aérien des deux pays. L’article Niger : arrivée d'un premier convoi algérien de 7 camions pour trois projets est apparu en premier sur TSA .

items.title: Ogou/Environnement : La DRE Plateaux-Est reboise le site de ses locaux
items.description: Atakpamé, 21 juil. (ATOP) – Les agents de la Direction régionale de l’éducation (DRE) Plateaux-Est ont reboisé la cour de leur direction, le mardi 21 juillet à Atakpamé. L’activité se situe dans le cadre de la promotion et la protection de l’environnement pour le développement durable. L’objectif est d’offrir un cadre de travail verdoyant et apaisant aux agents tout en rendant ce service public plus accueillant pour les usagers et visiteurs. Au total, 151 jeunes plants de diverses essences ont été mis en terre sur l’esplanade principale de la DRE, les bordures de la voie de désenclavement récemment aménagée et la cour intérieure du service. Pour ce troisième espace, le personnel s’est particulièrement mobilisé en apportant individuellement des plants floraux afin de constituer un bac à fleurs collectif. Le préfet de l’Ogou, Ekpé Kodjo Agbéko, a souligné que cette action s’inscrit en parfaite cohérence avec la vision environnementale et la politique nationale de reboisement insufflées par le gouvernement et le Président du Conseil, Faure Gnassingbé. Le directeur régional de l’éducation des Plateaux-Est, Ouro Bagna Fousseni, a exhorté l’ensemble de ses collaborateurs à s’investir non seulement dans la plantation, mais surtout dans le suivi et l’entretien régulier de ces plants. Il a ajouté que la mise en terre des plants constitue une empreinte positive et durable laissée par chaque agent au service de la collectivité et de l’administration. ATOP/KKT/MG/KYA

items.title: Nadra official goes missing in Karachi, family approaches police for his recovery
items.description: KARACHI: The family of a missing National Database and Registration Authority (Nadra) official approached police on Tuesday with an application for his recovery. The application, seen by Dawn , addressed Sindh Inspector General of Police (IG) Javed Alam Odho and stated that applicant Sohail Sangi’s younger son, Fayyaz Ahmed Janjhi, who is an assistant director at Nadra, had been missing since 6:30pm on July 20. Sangi, a veteran journalist and a member of the Human Rights Commission of Pakistan, further stated that his family had tried to contact Janjhi continuously, but his phone was switched off. The family also contacted his colleagues, who said he had left his office at Ayesha Manzil at around 6pm on Monday, he added. “But he did not reach home,” the application said. Urging authorities to register a complaint of him going missing and locate him, the applicant said his son or the rest of the family had no enmity with anyone. Janjhi’s elder brother, Riaz Sohail, who is also a senior journalist at BBC , told Dawn that the family had approached the Sindh IG with the application, and the provincial police chief had assigned the case to Central Senior Superintendent of Police Imran Khan. The Human Rights Commission of Pakistan (HRCP) also posted on X: “HRCP is deeply concerned to learn that Fayaz Janjhi, a Nadra officer and son of HRCP council member Sohail Sangi, has been missing since yesterday. We demand that the Sindh government expedite their efforts to recover Mr Janjhi safely.” Karachi Press Club President Fazil Jamili posted on X that he, too, was concerned about Janjhi’s disappearance and urged the Sindh government and law enforcement agencies to take “immediate action and expedite efforts to ensure his safe and swift recovery”.

items.title: BFEM: un taux de réussite de 53,38% au premier tour
items.description: Le premier tour du Brevet de fin d'études moyennes (Bfem) 2026 s'est soldé par un taux d'admission national de 53,38%. Les statistiques publiées par la Direction des examens et concours (Dexco) mettent en évidence des performances contrastées selon les académies. Si plusieurs régions de l'intérieur enregistrent des résultats remarquables, les grands pôles urbains continuent d'afficher des taux de réussite inférieurs à la moyenne nationale. Les premiers résultats de l’examen du Bfem 2026 confirment une dynamique globalement positive. Sur 195.664 candidats inscrits, 192.856 ont effectivement pris part aux épreuves, soit un taux de présence de 98,56%. Selon la Direction des examens et concours (Dexco) qui a communiqué au « Soleil » les résultats, au terme des corrections du premier groupe, 102.943 candidats ont été déclarés admis, représentant 53,38% des présents. Les filles continuent de devancer légèrement les garçons avec un taux de réussite de 54,02%, contre 52,46% chez leurs camarades masculins. Dans certaines académies comme Kaffrine ou Sédhiou, les garçons réalisent néanmoins des performances supérieures, preuve que les dynamiques restent variables, selon les contextes locaux. Au-delà de ces premiers résultats, les perspectives demeurent encourageantes. Les statistiques montrent que 31,96% des candidats sont autorisés à subir les épreuves du second groupe. Selon le Directeur des examens et concours, Papa Baba Diassé, cette configuration laisse apparaître, à ce stade, une tendance globale de réussite de 84%, contre 78,90% lors de la session 2025, soit une progression de 5,10 points. Une estimation qui devra toutefois être confirmée à l'issue des épreuves du second groupe et de la publication des résultats définitifs. L'analyse des résultats révèle, cependant, un paysage contrasté. Les meilleures performances sont enregistrées par les académies de Kolda, qui affichent un taux de réussite de 74,82%, Kédougou (74,49%), Matam (72,81%) et Kaffrine (70,22%). Ces académies confirment les progrès observés ces dernières années dans plusieurs régions de l'intérieur, où les efforts consentis en matière d'encadrement pédagogique et de suivi des élèves semblent produire des résultats significatifs. À l'inverse, les académies concentrant les effectifs les plus importants continuent de tirer la moyenne nationale vers le bas. Thiès, qui compte 34.875 candidats inscrits, n'enregistre qu'un taux de réussite de 44,07%. Pikine-Guédiawaye, avec plus de 27.000 candidats, obtient 44,40%, tandis que Rufisque (43,82%) présente le taux le plus faible du pays. Fatick (48,16%) demeure également sous la barre des 50%. L’Académie de Kolda en tête avec 74,82% de réussite Ces résultats rappellent les défis auxquels restent confrontés les grands bassins scolaires, où les effectifs élevés rendent souvent plus complexe le suivi pédagogique des élèves. Les autres académies affichent des performances plus proches ou supérieures à la moyenne nationale. Selon la Dexco, Tambacounda enregistre 65,79% de réussite, devant Sédhiou (64,07%), Ziguinchor (61,45%), Saint-Louis (59,67%), Kaolack (58,47%), Louga (54,54%), Dakar (54,41%) et Diourbel (54,02%). Cette répartition traduit des écarts parfois importants entre les territoires et souligne les marges de progression qui subsistent dans certaines académies. Selon la Dexco, les statistiques mettent également en évidence la forte mobilisation des candidats. Sur l'ensemble du territoire, seuls 2.808 candidats ont été absents, soit un taux de présence de 98,56%. Plusieurs académies, notamment Dakar, affichent même des taux de présence supérieurs à 99%, témoignant de la bonne organisation de l'examen et de l'engagement des candidats. Cette session marque également une avancée importante dans la modernisation de la publication des résultats. Selon le Directeur des examens et concours, Papa Baba Diassé, les traditionnelles scènes d'attroupement devant les centres d'examen appartiennent désormais au passé. Grâce au code QR intégré aux convocations, les candidats ont pu consulter leurs résultats directement depuis leur domicile, à l'aide de leur téléphone portable, mais aussi télécharger et imprimer leur relevé de notes en ligne. Cette innovation s'inscrit dans la stratégie de digitalisation des examens nationaux engagée par le ministère de l'Éducation nationale et vise à offrir un service plus rapide, plus sécurisé et plus accessible aux candidats. Le rendez-vous est déjà fixé pour les candidats admissibles. Dans un communiqué publié samedi dernier, le ministère de l'Éducation nationale annonce que les épreuves du second groupe se dérouleront le mardi 21 juillet 2026 à partir de 7 h 30 sur l'ensemble du territoire national, ainsi qu'en Gambie et en Arabie saoudite. Les candidats autorisés à y prendre part sont invités à se présenter dans leurs centres d'examen munis de leur convocation et d'une pièce d'identité en cours de validité. Cette ultime étape permettra de connaître le taux définitif de réussite de la session 2026 du Bfem. Daouda DIOUF

items.title: El empresario Julio Martínez se ratifica ante el juez al señalar a Zapatero por el rescate de Plus Ultra, pero afirma que no sabe con quién habló
items.description: El empresario Julio Martínez Martínez ha ratificado este martes ante el juez José Luis Calama, instructor del caso Plus Ultra en la Audiencia Nacional, el escrito que envió el lunes al magistrado para señalar a José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero como la persona que “marcaba los pasos a seguir” en las gestiones que desembocaron en el rescate de 53 millones de euros que la compañía aérea recibió del Gobierno de Pedro Sánchez tras la pandemia de coronavirus. Varias fuentes presentes en el interrogatorio inciden en que el imputado, que ha declarado durante cerca de tres horas tras imprimir un giro de guion a su estrategia y ofrecerse a “colaborar” con la justicia, ha implicado al expresidente en los trabajos para la aerolínea, pero ha sostenido que no sabe si el antiguo jefe del Ejecutivo intermedió ante alguien para agilizar la ayuda. items.description: A Mopti, le prix de la viande a connu une hausse samedi dernier 18 juillet. Le kilogramme de la viande avec os est passe de 3500 Fcfa a 4000 Fcfa, alors que celle sans os a grimpe de 4000 Fcfa a 4500 Fcfa le kilo. Selon les bouchers cette augmentation est liee a l'insecurite et a la rarete du betail dans la ville de Mopti. items.title: Un joyero que mató a tiros a dos atracadores, nuevo héroe de la derecha italiana
items.description: Hay muchos nervios en la derecha italiana del Gobierno de Giorgia Meloni a un año de las elecciones, si no se adelantan a primavera, ante el ascenso del nuevo líder ultra, el antiguo general Roberto Vannacci , que acusa al Ejecutivo de ser muy blando y que en los sondeos ya obtiene un 7% de los votos, por encima de la Liga de Matteo Salvini. Por eso la competición surge ante cualquier hecho de actualidad que se preste a demostrar quién es más extremista. El último ha sido la condena definitiva a 14 años y nueve meses de prisión a un joyero de Grinzane Cavour, en la región de Piamonte, por matar a tiros en 2021 a dos asaltantes y herir a un tercero, a los que siguió con una pistola en la calle cuando huían y disparó por la espalda. Las imágenes grabadas en vídeo son claras. Seguir leyendo

items.title: KSE-100 makes marginal gains as global energy uncertainty weighs on early rally
items.description: The Pakistan Stock Exchange’s (PSX) benchmark index on Tuesday made marginal gains as investor sentiment remained cautious over uncertainty surrounding global energy markets. The KSE-100 index opened in the green, soaring by 2,442.72 points to reach an intraday high of 178,370.45 at 10:09am. However, the market dipped sharply from 12pm onwards, dropping below the 177,000-point level at 1:56pm. It then finally closed at 176,133.56 points, up by mere 205.83 points (0.12 per cent) from the previous close. Topline Securities Ltd noted that the “strong start proved short-lived, as participants opted to lock in gains at elevated levels, triggering broad-based profit-taking during the latter half of the session”. “The subdued close reflected cautious investor sentiment amid lingering geopolitical tensions and persistent uncertainty surrounding global energy markets, prompting market participants to remain selective despite the early optimism,” it added. Topline further observed that refinery stocks bore the brunt of the selling pressure amid “market speculation that the long-awaited refinery policy could face further delays”. “Overall, investor sentiment remained guarded as market participants continued to assess geopolitical developments and their potential implications for global oil prices, inflation, and foreign investment flows,” the brokerage house said. Earlier in the day, KSE-100 had witnessed a strong rally as markets weighed reports of mediation efforts between the US and Iran against an exchange of fresh attacks between the two. As of 1:15pm PKT, global oil prices had fallen by more than 1pc. However, the majority of the early market gains eroded as oil prices rose by nearly 2pc, following continued hostilities in the Gulf as well as threats of a naval blockade of Saudi Arabia by Yemen’s Houthis. Brent crude futures rose $1.62, or 1.8pc, to $90.84 a barrel by 4:52pm PKT. The front-month US West Texas Intermediate crude contract, which expires on Tuesday, was up $1.37, or 1.7pc, at $84.60. The more active contract for September delivery rose $1.48, or 1.8pc, to $83.96. Awais Ashraf, director of research at AKD Securities, said the market extended the previous session’s late recovery as “oil prices retreated from a one-month high amid renewed mediation efforts between the US and Iran”. “Investor sentiment is also being supported by optimism surrounding the ongoing earnings season, with oil and gas exploration, cement, refinery and textile companies expected to post stronger-than-usual results,” he said. The stock exchange had opened the week on Monday on a depressed note, with the market remaining under extreme volatility. However, late value-hunting allowed the benchmark KSE-100 index to close the session in the green with a meagre gain. Additional input from Reuters

items.title: Football : avec Zidane, un nouveau staff pourrait se dessiner chez les Bleus
items.description: Après 14 ans sous l’ère Didier Deschamps, l’équipe de France s’apprête à ouvrir une nouvelle page. Sauf improbable retournement, Zinédine Zidane devrait prendre les commandes des Bleus jusqu’en 2030, avec l’Euro 2028 et le Mondial 2030 en ligne de mire. Pour l’accompagner, Zidane devrait s’appuyer sur un entourage proche, composé de collaborateurs avec lesquels il a déjà travaillé, notamment lors de son passage au Real Madrid.
